fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In the context of semantics, the principle of compositionality holds that the meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its constituents and the way they enter into the structure of that complex expression.

A consequence of compositionality is substitutivity: if two expressions have the same meaning, then one can be substituted for the other in any context without changing the meaning of expressions into which one or the other enters. Substitutivity is intensional in nature. Consider that if it were purely extensional, this would lead to changes in meaning, thus violating the very principle of compositionality. For instance, the extensions of "organisms with a heart" and "organisms with a kidney" are the same, but substituting one for the other in the sentence "all organisms with a heart are organisms with a kidney" can produce four sentences with different meanings:
All organisms with a heart are organisms with a kidney.
All organisms with a kidney are organisms with a heart.
All organisms with a heart are organisms with a heart.
All organisms with a kidney are organisms with a kidney.
The last two sentences are tautological, but the first two are not. Furthermore, the first two sentences are converses of each other.

The denotational semantics defines the meanings of terms in a language T using a valuation function that maps syntactic terms in the syntactic domain T to semantic values in the semantic domain V. Usually, we enclose expressions in semantic brackets to express their denotation, e.g., for term e, we can write
\llbracket e \rrbracket
such that \llbracket - \rrbracket : T \to V.

The valuation function is compositional; the meaning of a compound term is defined purely in terms of the meanings of its subterms. This compositionality simplifies reasoning, allowing us to replace "equals by equals", i.e., we can substitute terms with other terms having the same meaning.
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket }{\llbracket f(x) \rrbracket = \llbracket f(x') \rrbracket }

Because terms and their semantics are built up inductively, proofs about denotation semantics typically proceed using structural induction. For instance, we can prove that our expression semantics is total: for every expression e in the language, there is a value v such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = v. The proof of totality proceeds by induction on the structure of the expression e.

Structural induction is a syntax-directed proof style that defines a base case and an inductive case. The base case provides a term for which a property P holds, while the inductive case shows how—assuming P holds for some arbitrary subterm(s)—it also holds for a composition that includes these subterms.

Consider the case where you have an small addition language. Suppose we wish to prove that the denotational semantics of the language is total, that is, that that for every expression e there is an integer n such that \llbracket e \rrbracket = n. If we assume compositionality, we can prove totality holds using structural induction.

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }
The base case is the constructor e = \text {Val}\ n.
This case is trivial given the semantics, specifically \llbracket \text {Val}\ n \rrbracket = n.
The inductive case is the constructor e = \text {Add}\ x\ y.

Assume \llbracket x \rrbracket = n and \llbracket y \rrbracket = m for some integers n and m. It follows from the valuation function that
\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket x \rrbracket + \llbracket y \rrbracket = n + m
By assuming that the subterms of \text {Add} already satisfy totality—the so-called inductive hypothesis—we can prove that totality also holds for \text {Add}\ x\ y.

fr:title.text: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The compositionality property of the addition language

fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
The compositionality of the language may be defined as follows:
\frac {\llbracket x \rrbracket = \llbracket x' \rrbracket \ \llbracket y \rrbracket = \llbracket y' \rrbracket } {\llbracket \text {Add}\ x\ y \rrbracket = \llbracket \text {Add}\ x'\ y' \rrbracket }
In computer science, programming language semantics seeks to give precise mathematical meaning to programs.
